postText:
9 березня я завершую свій шлях у Devlight.

Рік тому я прийшов як iOS Department Lead, а згодом отримав промоушн на позицію Head of Mobile.
Цей шлях став для мене історією про те, як технологічна трансформація змінює бізнес-результати.

Сьогодні я йду, залишаючи систему, яка працює автономно. Але головний результат для мене — це зміна культури та мислення команди.

Що вдалося реалізувати:

Від «виконавців» до партнерів.
Усі інженери iOS/Flutter-департаменту сьогодні мислять категоріями продукту. Команда перестала просто писати код за ТЗ — сьогодні вони ставлять критичні запитання, надають пропозиції та дбають про бізнес-результат як справжні партнери. Ми впровадили культуру Continuous Improvement та Continuous Learning: кожен процес проходив цикл «пропозиція — валідація — впровадження — фідбек». Інженери стали співавторами системи, а не просто користувачами гайдлайнів.

AI-driven інженерія.
Ми інтегрували AI-інструменти в повсякденну роботу — від аналізу коду до оптимізації рутинних процесів. Це дозволило сфокусувати більше часу на архітектурних рішеннях та якості, а не на рутинних задачах.

Mobile DNA та масштабування.
Ми вивели інженерні стандарти (архітектура, безпека, SDLC) на новий рівень. На рівні Mobile-департаменту ми ініціювали розширення CI/CD для Flutter-напрямку, що забезпечило QA-команду артефактами для підключення AI-інструментів у процес тестування.

Я вдячний CTO за підтримку підходу, де інженерія є повноцінним партнером продукту.

Компанія переходить у фазу активного масштабування. Я мотивований ролями, де потрібно запускати або системно розвивати інженерні функції.

Мій фокус — Engineering Excellence: створення стійких культур, де код стає інтелектуальним драйвером продукту.
Фундамент у Devlight закладено — і він працює.

Я відкритий до нових можливостей на позиціях:
Head of Mobile / Engineering Manager / Engineering Lead.

Там, де інженерія має бути стратегічним партнером бізнесу.

Devlight — дякую за цей рік.

Команда, продовжуйте ставити правильні запитання і не зупиняйтеся в інноваціях! 🚀

Stop letting "Blob" PRs kill your App Performance

💁‍♀️ Follow me for more technical insights on mobile engineering, CI/CD, and performance optimization.

We’ve all been there. You ship a "small" feature, but somehow the app bundle size jumps by 10MB.
By the time you notice it in production, it’s too late. Your conversion rates drop because of slow download times, and your team is stuck playing "detective" through old commits to find the culprit.
Sentry just solved this.
They’ve introduced Size Analysis Integration into CI, and it’s a game-changer for anyone building iOS or Android apps.

I’ve seen the pain of last-minute mobile build surprises, and that’s why integrating size analysis into CI isn’t a “nice-to-have”; it’s a must-have.
It transforms app size from a passive metric into an active quality gate, helping teams catch regressions early, enforce performance budgets, and ship smarter.

Shipping fast should never mean shipping bloated apps.

By bringing size awareness into CI, we shift quality left, prevent silent growth, and stay in control of our mobile performance from build to store release.
We already automate tests and security. It’s time we automate performance budgets too.

Every time a developer writes code, this kicks in automatically. Here's what it does.

🔵 One Tech Term #8

CI/CD — Continuous Integration & Continuous Delivery

In plain English: CI/CD is like a factory conveyor belt for software. Every time a developer adds new code, it automatically gets tested, built, and prepared for release without anyone having to do it manually. No waiting for a big monthly deployment. No crossing fingers hoping nothing breaks. Small updates ship safely and frequently — sometimes dozens of times a day. Think of it as the difference between releasing one massive album once a year versus dropping singles every week.

Why it matters: CI/CD directly impacts how fast your team can ship value to users. When engineers talk about "pipeline failures," "deployment frequency," or "release cycles" — they're talking about CI/CD. As a PM or TPM, understanding this helps you set realistic timelines, diagnose slowdowns, and push for faster, safer delivery.

No more endless manual deployments! 🚀

After several iterations (and a few battles with Apple and Google configurations 😅), the CI/CD architecture for @Suntro is finally up and running, fully automated on GitHub Actions.

When building a cross-platform mobile app, build and deployment times can quickly become a bottleneck. Here is how I structured our pipeline to combine safety and speed:
🛡️ 1. The Gatekeeper (Analyze & Test): The crucial first step. If static analysis or a single test fails, everything stops. We don’t waste build minutes on broken code. 

⚡ 2. Massive Parallelization: Once the code is validated, we simultaneously trigger the AAB (Android) build, the IPA (iOS) build, AND the Shorebird release preparation for our future over-the-air (OTA) updates. 

📦 3. Continuous Deployment: As soon as the builds are ready, they are automatically shipped to the Google Play Console and Apple TestFlight.

The result? A monumental time saver and significantly reduced mental load. I can now focus 100% on shipping value (especially our family "Clan" sync 🐾) instead of watching loading bars.

What does your mobile pipeline look like? Are you team GitHub Actions, Fastlane, or Codemagic? 👇

CI/CD, aka Continuous Integration and Continuous Delivery, is your pipeline to get new software to users quickly and reliably. It's all about automating the build, test, and deployment process, so you can ship code faster and with fewer headaches.

A typical CI/CD pipeline has several connected stages:

➟ Developer commits code changes to source control (such Git)

➟ CI server detects changes and triggers build 

➟ Code is compiled, tested (unit, integration tests) 

➟ Test results reported to developer 

➟ On success, artifacts are deployed to staging environments 

➟ Further testing may be done on staging before release (e.g., User Acceptance Testing, Performance Testing) 

➟ CD system deploys approved changes to production (Manual approvals required in few cases)

➟ Continuous Monitoring of the application in production

postText:
Most teams stop at automated builds and call it “mobile CI/CD”. That’s the easy part.

The real pain shows up later:
1. Store submissions still happen manually
2. Hotfixes wait days for reviews
3. Release branches drift from what users actually run
4. Every “small fix” feels risky

Expo EAS gets interesting after builds. When:
1. eas submit removes Transporter + Play Console gymnastics
2. OTA updates turn small bugs into minutes, not weeks
3. eas.json becomes a release contract, not config noise

At that point, releases stop being events and start being infrastructure.
This post goes deep into:
1. automating submissions end-to-end
2. using OTA updates safely (not recklessly)
3. designing workflows that scale past “one app, one dev”

Read the full blog by Pushkar Thakur: https://lnkd.in/g_TgadmJ

postText:
Continuous Integration and Continuous Deployment (CI/CD) are crucial in modern software development, but Amazon and Google view CI/CD in unique ways that align with their distinct operational philosophies.

Amazon focuses on decentralization, empowering individual teams with autonomy to innovate and deploy swiftly. In contrast, Google emphasizes centralized efficiency, leveraging its robust infrastructure to streamline and standardize deployments.

Embracing flexibility and adaptability in CI/CD practices can lead to significant advancements in software delivery.

A Mobile Developer’s Guide to Scaling Side Projects


Building an app is easy.
Scaling it is the hard part.

Most side projects break the moment real users show up -> slow APIs, crashes, no analytics, no CI/CD.

If you want your project to look like a real product, here’s where to start:

1. 𝗛𝗼𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 & 𝗕𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗲𝗻𝗱
• Firebase / Supabase → serverless and quick to launch
• Cloud Functions → add custom logic when needed

2. 𝗗𝗮𝘁𝗮𝗯𝗮𝘀𝗲
• Firestore → flexible, fast iteration
• Postgres + Prisma → structured and reliable for scale

3. 𝗔𝘂𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻

• Apple & Google login → smoother onboarding, fewer drop-offs

4. 𝗖𝗿𝗮𝘀𝗵 𝗥𝗲𝗽𝗼𝗿𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 & 𝗟𝗼𝗴𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴
• Crashlytics → track crashes instantly
• Sentry → deeper monitoring and error logs

5. 𝗔𝗻𝗮𝗹𝘆𝘁𝗶𝗰𝘀
• Firebase Analytics → user behavior
• Mixpanel → funnels & retention
• Appsflyer → attribution for growth

• GitHub Actions / Bitrise → automate builds and testing
• Fastlane → seamless store deployments

7. 𝗠𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 & 𝗙𝗲𝗲𝗱𝗯𝗮𝗰𝗸

Side projects that scale show more than coding skills.

They show engineering depth, product thinking, and the ability to build for real-world users.

That’s what makes the difference between “just an app” and a production-ready product.

Daily Android Tip #52

Automate your builds with CI — don’t rely on manual APK/AAB generation.

Manual builds are:
 • Error-prone
 • Inconsistent
 • Hard to reproduce
 • Slow as the team grows

A CI pipeline (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Bitrise, CircleCI) ensures every commit is built, tested, and validated the same way.

⸻

What you should automate
 • Build Debug & Release variants
 • Run unit tests
 • Run lint / static checks
 • Generate signed AABs (with secrets)
 • Upload artifacts (optional)

⸻

Simple GitHub Actions example

name: Android CI

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
 build:
 runs-on: ubuntu-latest
 steps:
 - uses: actions/checkout@v3
 - uses: actions/setup-java@v3
 with:
 distribution: 'zulu'
 java-version: 17
 - name: Build
 run: ./gradlew assembleDebug


⸻

Why this matters
 • Catches issues early
 • Prevents “works on my machine” problems
 • Improves team confidence
 • Speeds up releases
 • Makes scaling easier

⸻

Common mistakes
 • Building releases only on local machines
 • Not running tests in CI
 • Storing keystore files in repo
 • Skipping CI for “small changes”

⸻

Thumb Rule:

If a build step is important — automate it.
If it’s manual — it will eventually break.

postText:
Best Practices for Scalable App Development – Avoiding Technical Debt Early

Scalability isn’t just about handling more users—it’s about building apps that grow without breaking. Too often, developers cut corners to ship fast, leading to technical debt that slows innovation and increases maintenance costs.

Here’s how to avoid technical debt early and build apps that scale seamlessly:

🔥 Best Practices for Scalable Development

✅ Design for Scalability from Day One – Use modular architecture (e.g., microservices or feature-driven development) to keep your codebase clean and adaptable.

✅ Prioritize Clean Code & Documentation – Future-proof your app with clear, maintainable code and proper documentation so teams can onboard quickly.

✅ Use the Right State Management – In Flutter, choose between Riverpod, Bloc, or Provider depending on your app’s complexity. Poor state management can lead to performance bottlenecks.

✅ Leverage Cloud-Native Solutions – Use Google Cloud, Firebase, or AWS Lambda to handle traffic spikes efficiently without over-provisioning servers.

✅ Optimize Your Database – Choose a scalable database (e.g., Firestore, PostgreSQL, or Supabase) and avoid overusing real-time listeners that can drain performance.

✅ Automate Testing & CI/CD – Implement unit, integration, and load testing early. Set up CI/CD pipelines (e.g., GitHub Actions, Bitrise) to catch issues before deployment.

✅ Monitor Performance & Logs – Use Sentry, Firebase Performance Monitoring, or Datadog to track errors and optimize app speed in real time.


---

Building a Flutter app is great…

Think of CI/CD as your auto-pilot mode for app development.
 It automatically:
Builds your Flutter app
Runs tests
Checks for errors
Creates APK/IPA files
And even publishes updates
No manual steps. No headaches. No last-minute mistakes.

Because it gives you:
 ✅ Faster development
 ✅ Fewer bugs
 ✅ Automated builds
 ✅ Smooth releases

🧱 3️⃣ Simple CI/CD Workflow for Flutter
The easiest workflow looks like this:
Push code to GitHub/GitLab
CI automatically triggers
It installs Flutter SDK
It builds the Android/iOS app
Runs tests
Generates app files (APK/IPA)
Sends them to you or uploads to store
You write code → CI/CD does the rest.

CI/CD is not just a “DevOps thing.”

Stop treating your Production builds like a game of Russian Roulette. 🎲
I’ve seen it happen too many times: a "quick manual fix" leads to a broken release, a mismatched version code, or worse—a debug build hitting the Play Store.
If you're still uploading builds directly from your local machine, you aren't just saving time; you're accumulating technical debt.
The "Manual Upload" Trap: Environment Drift: "It works on my machine" is a dangerous baseline. 
 Human Error: Accidentally shipping the wrong branch or a build with skipped ProGuard rules. 
 Security Flaws: Managing keystores and API keys locally is a disaster waiting to happen. 
 Testing Gaps: When things get busy, manual testing is the first thing that gets compromised.
This is why CI/CD is a non-negotiable in 2026: ⚡
Immutable Environments: Every build happens in a clean, standardized container. Period.
Automated Gatekeepers: Unit tests, UI tests, and Lint checks run on every PR. If it’s broken, it doesn't merge.
Encrypted Secrets: Keystores and sensitive keys stay in the cloud, encrypted and automated.
Deployment Velocity: One click (or one merge) and your build is out. No stress, no manual monitoring.
CI/CD doesn't slow you down—it gives you the confidence to move faster without breaking the app. 🚀

🌬️ Breathing Intelligence Into IoT: AI-Powered Air Quality Monitoring with Raspberry Pi & ESP32

At Metallicode, we believe the future of smart environments doesn't always live in the cloud — sometimes it runs right on the edge, in your hands, on a $10 microcontroller.

We've been exploring how to bring AI directly into local IoT projects for air quality monitoring, and the results have been eye-opening. Here's what we've learned:

🔬 The Setup
Using ESP32 sensors (MQ-135, SGP30, BME280) for real-time data capture and a Raspberry Pi as the local inference hub, we built a pipeline that detects pollutants, predicts air quality trends, and triggers alerts — entirely offline.

🧠 Why Run AI Locally?
✅ No cloud dependency — works in remote or restricted environments
✅ Ultra-low latency decisions (critical for health alerts)
✅ Data privacy — sensitive environmental data stays on-device
✅ Cost-efficient at scale — no API fees per inference

⚙️ What AI Brings to the Table
With a lightweight TensorFlow Lite or ONNX model running on the Raspberry Pi, we can move beyond simple threshold alerts and into predictive analytics — flagging degrading air quality before it becomes dangerous, recognizing pollution patterns, and even correlating with external data like weather or traffic.

🔧 The Stack
- ESP32 → sensor data over MQTT
- Raspberry Pi → local ML inference (TFLite)
- SQLite → local data logging
- Grafana → real-time dashboard (local network)

This kind of embedded AI is exactly where we see the next wave of innovation for industrial monitoring, smart agriculture, and urban sensing.

I've moved data with Fivetran, broken things with Airbyte, and underestimated Stitch. 

Here's what nobody tells you about managed vs. open-source connectors. 🧵

Most data teams pick their ELT tool based on a blog post from 2021. Here's the real-world breakdown after working across all three:

🔵 Fivetran — The "Just Works" Premium
If your company is scaling fast and engineer time is expensive, Fivetran pays for itself.

→ Connectors are maintained, schema drift is handled automatically, and SLAs are real.

→ The catch? Cost scales with Monthly Active Rows (MAR). Forecast your volume before you commit.

🟢 Airbyte — The Builder's Playground
Open-source with a cloud option. This is where data engineers actually learn the craft.

→ You can fork a connector, write your own with the Connector Development Kit, or self-host the whole thing.

→ Ideal when you need flexibility over polish. Expect to invest ops time if you go self-hosted.

🟠 Stitch (by Talend) — The Underrated Workhorse

Built on Singer, the open-source tap/target protocol — meaning connectors are portable.

→ Fastest time-to-pipeline for standard sources. Low overhead, predictable pricing.

→ Often overlooked because it doesn't have a flashy community. Don't sleep on it for lean teams.

The real decision matrix:

• Speed to production? → Fivetran
• Custom sources + cost control? → Airbyte
• Small team, standard stack? → Stitch

What most people miss: the connector tool is only 20% of the problem. The other 80% is schema design, orchestration, and data contracts downstream.

I've seen Fivetran contracts fail because the warehouse wasn't modeled properly. And I've seen Airbyte on a $0 budget outperform managed tools — because the team understood their data.

Your stack doesn't define your maturity. Your reasoning does.

⚠️ 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗺𝗼𝗻 𝗠𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲𝘀 𝗗𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀 𝗠𝗮𝗸𝗲 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗙𝗹𝘂𝘁𝘁𝗲𝗿 𝗙𝗹𝗮𝘃𝗼𝗿𝘀 (𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗛𝗼𝘄 𝘁𝗼 𝗔𝘃𝗼𝗶𝗱 𝗧𝗵𝗲𝗺)
Working with Flutter Flavors can make your life easier—or much harder.

After building multi-client apps and manage env-based builds, I’ve seen the same flavor-related mistakes slow down even the most talented devs.

Let’s break them down:

❌ 𝗠𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲 #𝟭: 𝗛𝗮𝗿𝗱𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗳𝗶𝗴𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝗺𝗮𝗶𝗻.𝗱𝗮𝗿𝘁
Why it hurts: Makes switching environments a nightmare.
✅ Fix: Use --dart-define and load config at runtime. Keep secrets and endpoints in separate env files.

❌ 𝗠𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲 #𝟮: 𝗜𝗴𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱.𝗴𝗿𝗮𝗱𝗹𝗲 & 𝗫𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝘀𝗲𝘁𝘂𝗽𝘀
Why it hurts: Your flavor build fails or uses the wrong assets.
✅ Fix: Properly configure productFlavors (Android) & build schemes (iOS). Test each before CI/CD.

❌ 𝗠𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲 #𝟯: 𝗢𝗻𝗲 𝗕𝗮𝗰𝗸𝗲𝗻𝗱 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗷𝗲𝗰𝘁 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝗮𝗹𝗹 𝗳𝗹𝗮𝘃𝗼𝗿𝘀
Why it hurts: Mixing dev data with production. Risky & messy.
✅ Fix: Create one Backend project per flavor (Dev, Stage, Prod).

❌ 𝗠𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲 #𝟰: 𝗡𝗼 𝗖𝗜/𝗖𝗗 𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗺𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻
Why it hurts: Manual builds = inconsistent, error-prone deployments.
✅ Fix: Automate builds per flavor using GitHub Actions, Bitrise, or Codemagic. Use env variables smartly.

❌ 𝗠𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗮𝗸𝗲 #𝟱: 𝗡𝗼𝘁 𝘀𝗲𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗲𝘁𝘀 𝗼𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗲𝗺𝗲𝘀
Why it hurts: Branding conflicts across clients or versions.
✅ Fix: Dynamically load logos, colors, app names via configs and flavor-specific folders.

🧠 Flutter Flavors are powerful—but only when done right. If you’re building a multi-environment or white-label app, make sure your architecture supports it cleanly.

✅ DeployGate – A simple OTA (Over-the-Air) distribution service for testing iOS and Android apps.
✅ Appaloosa – A mobile application management platform that allows private app distribution for enterprises.
✅ AppBox – A great choice for developers who need a quick way to distribute iOS apps via OTA links.

postText:
מלחמת ה-Agents הגדולה: Claude Code vs, Google Antigravity vs, Codex GPT מי מהם באמת יחליף לכם את ה-IDE?
עזבו אתכם מ-Autocomplete אנחנו כבר לא שם ב-2026, המאבק הוא על מי האג'נט שייקח את ה-Jira Ticket שלכם ויהפוך אותו ל-Pull Request מוכן ל-Production בזמן שאתם שותים קפה.
אחרי בדיקה של שלוש המפלצות החדשות בשוק, הנה המפה המלאה שתעזור לכם להחליט איפה להשקיע את זמן הלמידה שלכם:
1. Claude Code 
הכלי של Anthropic הוא הבחירה של ה-Purists. מדובר בכלי CLI (שורת פקודה) שמבין קוד ברמת עומק שלא הכרנו.
החוזקה: יכולת Reasoning (הסקה) פנומנלית. הוא לא רק כותב קוד, הוא מבין למה הקוד נכתב ככה. הוא מצטיין בשינויים רוחביים ב-Large-scale Repositories.
רמת ביצוע: הכי גבוהה בכתיבת קוד נקי (Clean Code) ומניעת באגים לוגיים.
2. Google Antigravity 
גוגל הלכו על חוויית ה-IDE המלאה. זה לא רק כותב קוד, זה אקו-סיסטם שלם של אג'נטים.
החוזקה: מולטי-מודאליות ואוטומציה. Antigravity יכול להריץ דפדפן, "לראות" שהכפתור יצא מהמקום ב-CSS, ולתקן אותו לבד. הוא מנצח בכל מה שקשור ל-Testing ו-Deployment.
רמת ביצוע: מהירות Delivery חסרת תקדים לפרויקטים מקצה לקצה (Full-stack).
3. Codex GPT 
המענה של OpenAI (האבולוציה של Codex/Canvas) הוא הכלי הכי "אנושי" בחבורה. הוא יודע לקחת דרישות עמומות ולהפוך אותן למציאות.
החוזקה: פתרון בעיות יצירתי ואינטגרציה. הוא הכי טוב בלקחת פרומפט חצי-אפוי ולהבין בדיוק למה התכוונתם. בנוסף, הוא נהנה מה-Knowledge Base הכי רחב שיש בעולם ה-AI.
רמת ביצוע: מנצח במהירות ה-Prototyping. אם אתם צריכים להקים משהו מאפס או לממש אלגוריתם מורכב וחדש הוא שם.
השורה התחתונה שלי:
אם אתם עובדים על מערכת קריטית שבה כל באג עולה הון – Claude Code הוא השותף שלכם.
אם אתם במירוץ להוציא פיצ'רים ב-Scale מטורף – Antigravity יחסוך לכם חצי יום עבודה.
ואם אתם צריכים שותף לסיעור מוחות טכנולוגי ומימוש מהיר של רעיונות – Codex GPT נשאר המלך של ה-Creative Coding.
התחזית שלי? המנצח הגדול הוא המפתח שיידע להשתמש בשלושתם לפי הצורך. הכלים הפכו מ"מקלדת חכמה" ל"צוות פיתוח" שלם בתוך הלפטופ.
באיזה צד של המפה אתם? כבר בחרתם פייבוריט או שאתם עדיין מחכים?
הכנתי מדריך ראשוני להתקנה של כל אחד מהכלים הגיבו ״מדריך״ בתגובה ואשלח את הקובץ:)

🚀 Flutter Full-Stack in 2025: What’s Powering Your Production Apps?
As Flutter matures into a stable, high-performance cross-platform framework, the real differentiator for production apps lies in how you architect your full stack — from state management to backend, CI/CD, and observability.
Over the past year, I’ve seen high-performing teams make strategic trade-offs in their tech stacks:
🔹 Firebase is unbeatable for MVP speed — but scaling comes with vendor lock-in, pricing complexity, and reduced backend control.
 🔹 Node.js + NestJS offers scalable, modular APIs that integrate well with Docker/Kubernetes, though initial setup is heavier.
 🔹 Django or FastAPI (Python) delivers fast iteration, rich libraries, and pairs naturally with PostgreSQL, Redis, or Celery queues.
 🔹 Supabase and Appwrite are disrupting the backend space with open-source, serverless alternatives — great for startups and fast pivots.
🧠 On the frontend, Flutter state management is maturing fast:
Riverpod is lightweight, composable, and testable — great for modern apps.
BLoC still dominates enterprise and layered architectures where predictability is key.
⚙️ CI/CD pipelines now often include:
GitHub Actions for native GitOps.
Codemagic and Bitrise for Flutter-specific automation.
Integrations with Firebase App Distribution, Play Console, and TestFlight streamline beta feedback.
📊 Observability is no longer optional:
Use tools like Sentry, Firebase Crashlytics, AppSignal, or Datadog for logging, performance monitoring, and crash diagnostics.
Real-time analytics (Mixpanel, Amplitude, Firebase) drive data-backed decisions.
💡 The takeaway?
 Choosing the right tools isn’t about trends — it’s about balancing speed, scale, and sustainability for your product’s stage and roadmap.
👉 I’d love to hear what YOUR full-stack Flutter architecture looks like in 2025. What tools or decisions made the biggest difference?

postText:
1. Programming Language: Kotlin
Focus on Kotlin, as it’s now the standard for Android development. Java is still useful for older projects, but Kotlin is the future.

2. UI Building: Jetpack Compose & XML
Jetpack Compose: The modern way to build UI in Android.
XML: Still important for maintaining legacy projects, but Compose is the future.

3. Networking: Fetching Data
Use Retrofit or Ktor for API requests.

4. Local Database
Learn Room for structured, modern local storage, though SQLite and Realm are alternatives.

5. DataStore
Use DataStore to persist key-value pairs (replaces SharedPreferences) for small datasets.

6. Architecture Patterns
MVVM: Most popular.
MVI, MVP, and MVC: Useful alternatives based on project needs.

7. Image Loading
Coil: Ideal for modern projects.
Glide and Picasso: Still common in legacy codebases.

8. Dependency Injection
Hilt: The standard DI tool.
Koin: Lightweight and Kotlin-native.

9. Coroutines
Learn Kotlin Coroutines for asynchronous tasks, a must-have for modern Android apps.

10. Code Quality
Use linters like Ktlint, Detekt, and Android Lint to maintain clean, error-free code.

11. Debugging Tools
Timber: Simplifies logging.
LeakCanary: Detects memory leaks.
Chuck: Network debugging.

12. Unit Testing
Use JUnit, Kluent, and MockK for thorough unit testing.

13. Instrumented Testing
Espresso: For UI tests.
Robolectric: Runs Android tests on the JVM.

14. CI/CD Tools
Bitrise: Mobile-centric CI/CD.
CircleCI: Cloud-based with flexibility.

postText:
💡 𝐈 𝐰𝐚𝐬 𝐣𝐮𝐬𝐭 𝐰𝐨𝐧𝐝𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐰𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐚𝐥𝐥 𝐜𝐚𝐦𝐞 𝐧𝐞𝐰 𝐢𝐧 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐚𝐮𝐭𝐨𝐦𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 𝐬𝐩𝐚𝐜𝐞…

That’s when I stumbled upon Maestro — a modern, lightweight mobile UI automation framework that’s becoming quite popular among testers and developers 🚀

Unlike traditional frameworks such as Appium, Espresso, or XCTest (which are powerful but often complex and flaky), Maestro is designed with simplicity and speed in mind:
🔹 𝑫𝒆𝒄𝒍𝒂𝒓𝒂𝒕𝒊𝒗𝒆 𝒀𝑨𝑴𝑳 𝒔𝒚𝒏𝒕𝒂𝒙 → You can write tests in plain, readable steps without heavy coding.
🔹 𝑪𝒓𝒐𝒔𝒔-𝒑𝒍𝒂𝒕𝒇𝒐𝒓𝒎 𝒔𝒖𝒑𝒑𝒐𝒓𝒕 → One framework works for Android, iOS, React Native, Flutter, and even WebViews.
🔹 𝑺𝒎𝒂𝒓𝒕 𝒘𝒂𝒊𝒕𝒔 𝒃𝒖𝒊𝒍𝒕-𝒊𝒏  → No need to add manual sleep() or retry logic, Maestro automatically handles flakiness.
🔹 𝑹𝒂𝒑𝒊𝒅 𝒊𝒕𝒆𝒓𝒂𝒕𝒊𝒐𝒏 → Since it’s interpreted, changes in test files are instantly reflected while running.
🔹 𝑴𝒂𝒆𝒔𝒕𝒓𝒐 𝑺𝒕𝒖𝒅𝒊𝒐 → A visual inspector that helps generate test flows interactively.
🔹 𝑬𝒙𝒕𝒆𝒏𝒔𝒊𝒃𝒍𝒆 𝒘𝒊𝒕𝒉 𝑱𝑺 → Add custom logic where needed
🔹 𝑪𝑰/𝑪𝑫 & 𝑪𝒍𝒐𝒖𝒅 𝒓𝒆𝒂𝒅𝒚 → Works with GitHub Actions, Jenkins, Bitrise, etc. Maestro Cloud even lets you run on simulators at scale.

It feels like automation is moving closer towards being: - 
- 𝐃𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐫-𝐟𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐥𝐲 (easy to integrate into pipelines),
- 𝐓𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐫-𝐟𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐥𝐲 (less flaky, easy to write), and
- 𝐁𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬-𝐟𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐧𝐝𝐥𝐲 (readable scenarios for collaboration).

Of course, Maestro is still relatively new — community and ecosystem are growing — but it definitely looks like a fresh alternative to Appium if your focus is speed, readability, and reduced flakiness.

👉 Have you tried Maestro yet?
 👉 What’s your take on it compared to Appium, Selenium, or other frameworks?

🛠️ We're building ReconEyes. But some tools deserve to exist beyond the platform.

For months, we've been producing CTI reports coordinated with European partners.

And every time, we run into the same problem: there is no open, standardised, self-hostable tool to structure an intelligence report according to proper frameworks — Admiralty Scale, MITRE ATT&CK, Diamond Model, TLP 2.0.

So we built it for ourselves. And we decided to make it available to everyone as open source.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
🔍 CTI Report Builder — A ReconEyes module, with a standalone open source version available outside the platform
┃ Currently in active development
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

An IOC without context is a bit like a car with a beautiful body — but no engine.
You see the shape. You don't know where it's going, or why it's there.

That's exactly the problem we're trying to solve.

📋 A CTI report editor built for analysts, CERTs, and judicial authorities.

✅ What it will deliver:

🧭 Built-in Admiralty Wizard
Each piece of information is rated on two axes: source reliability (A → F) and information credibility (1 → 6). The wizard walks the analyst through scoring every assertion — exactly as required by European cyber intelligence standards.

🎯 Interactive MITRE ATT&CK
TTP matrix embedded directly in the report. Technique selection, Navigator JSON export. No more juggling between tools.

💠 Structured Diamond Model
Adversary, infrastructure, capability, victim — all four axes modelled visually and directly linked to the report sections.

🔐 Native TLP 2.0
Every report, every section, every piece of information tagged TLP:CLEAR / GREEN / AMBER / RED from the moment it's written.

📤 Clean export
Structured DOCX, Navigator JSON for ATT&CK — ready to submit to institutional partners.

🐳 One-command deployment
docker compose up
That's it. Self-hostable, sovereign, no cloud dependency.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Why open source?

Because a poorly structured report is lost intelligence.
Because authorities and cyber teams shouldn't have to reinvent this format for every case.

And because standardising CTI production is a common good.

The version integrated into ReconEyes will go further — multi-case management, real-time collaboration, audit trail, IOC/OSINT integration.

If you're a CTI analyst, part of a CERT, a cyber unit, or simply curious: this is built for you, and we're building it now.

👇 We'd love to hear about the standards you use, what's missing from your current workflows, or what you'd want to see in a tool like this.

We're building this with the community. Feedback is part of the engine.

This year, the coveted 4FYN prize went to Biorce, a Spanish health tech company whose pioneering use of AI is transforming the way clinical trials are planned and executed. 

The 4YFN Awards at MWC Barcelona recognise startups driving progress across core technology sectors and those intersecting with telecommunications. The competition is regarded as one of the most influential showcases for early-stage companies, offering visibility, investment opportunities, and validation on a global stage. 

This year’s edition brought together 20 outstanding finalists, each representing a vital frontier of technological advancement:

🧊 Climate Tech
DR Scrap Recycling (China) – data-driven automation for the scrap industry.
Sensegrass (UK) – AI and IoT soil intelligence for smarter farming.
Skyfora (Finland) – AI-powered weather forecasting for extreme events.
URAPHEX (Spain) – sustainable, chemical-free water regeneration.

🧊Digital Horizons
AIM Intelligence (Korea) – AI security middleware for enterprise systems.
DeepKeep (Israel) – safeguarding AI models against emerging threats.
Enhans (Korea) – AI-driven commerce infrastructure for digital transactions.
NeuralTrust (Spain) – secure deployment of generative AI applications.

🧊Fintech
Dost (Spain) – AI workflows for finance operations.
DRUO (USA) – direct bank payments without cards.
Kistpay (Private) Limited (Pakistan) – smartphone access via instalment financing.
Spendbase (Ukraine) – unified SaaS and cloud cost management.

🧊Health Tech
AI Diagnostics (South Africa) – AI screening tools for TB detection.
Biorce (Spain) – AI agents streamlining clinical trial operations.
Medwise AI (UK) – evidence-based answers for clinicians.
Sycai Medical (Spain) – AI imaging for early pancreatic lesion detection.

🧊Mobile Frontiers
ConnectHear (Pakistan) – real-time sign language interpretation.
Kreios Space SL (Spain) – extending satellite lifetimes in low orbit.
OptAI Inc. (Korea) – optimising on-device AI performance.
ZIM Connections (UK) – simplified global connectivity via eSIM.

Amid this diverse and competitive field, Biorce proved its ability to tackle one of healthcare’s most pressing challenges: the inefficiency of clinical trials. By reducing time, cost, and complexity, the company’s platform promises to accelerate the arrival of new treatments and therapies, ultimately benefiting patients worldwide.

From climate resilience to digital security, fintech, health tech, and mobile frontiers, the awards highlight the breadth of innovation shaping tomorrow’s industries.
